steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a "which finding would provide evidence" question. Grasby et al. investigated whether perceived category similarity predicts consumers' likelihood of purchasing brand extensions. The decisive structure: they had consumers rate similarity and calculated probability changes — so evidence that similarity predicts purchase behavior must show those two measures moving together.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Consumers' ratings varied substantially by category pair, whereas the changes in probability were broadly similar for each pair.': This shows the two measures moving in opposite directions — high variation in one, low variation in the other — which is evidence that similarity does NOT predict purchase probability. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Consumers tended to purchase more products in categories where extended-brand pairs are found.': This compares categories with and without extended-brand pairs, not similarity ratings to probability changes. It does not address the investigated relationship at all. ✗
  • Choice C — 'There was a strong positive correlation between consumers' ratings and the changes in probability.': A strong positive correlation between similarity ratings and probability changes is exactly what "similarity predicts purchase likelihood" looks like — higher perceived similarity corresponds to a higher probability of cross-brand purchase. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Consumers' ratings and the changes in probability calculated by the researchers [truncated].': This choice is incomplete as presented; it describes no meaningful relationship and cannot serve as evidence of anything. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
